text: A polarized training intensity distribution (~77% low/<VT, 3% moderate/between thresholds, 20% high/>RCT) improved 10K race time by 5.0% over 10 weeks in recreational runners.
A threshold-focused training distribution (~46% low, 35% moderate/between thresholds, 19% high) improved 10K race time by 3.6% over 10 weeks in recreational runners.

Polarized training produced ~1.4 percentage points greater 10K improvement than threshold-focused training (~41 seconds faster), though the group-level difference did not reach statistical significance in the full sample of 30 runners.

Runners who most closely adhered to a polarized intensity distribution showed 1.29 standardized Cohen effect-size units greater improvement than those who adhered to a threshold distribution (P = .038).

Both polarized and threshold-focused training programs significantly improve 10K performance in recreational runners over a 10-week intervention, suggesting consistent training with structured intensity zones is beneficial regardless of exact distribution.

Training intensity was controlled using ventilatory threshold (VT) and respiratory compensation threshold (RCT) to define three zones, with heart rate monitoring used to enforce zone compliance during each session.
